coredata.dc:title: Multicolor carbon dots: Induced by sp2-sp 3 hybridized domains and their application in ion detection and WLED
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Weijie
Bai, Jingjing
Cai, Tingting
Li, Shijia
Pang, Ernan
Zhang, Hua
Li, Zhenzhong
coredata.dc:description: The heteroatom doping to achieve carbon dots has played a crucial part in exploring the fluorescence mechanism of multicolor emissions, which is closely related with the application development of such materials. Herein, a facile method was developed to synthesize three-color CDs via the co-doping of S and N elements with the assistance of ethanol solvent. The achieved CDs mainly emit blue, green and red lights, yet the photoluminescence quantum yields (FLQYs) generally decreases from 54.0% for B-CDs, 11.5% for G-CDs to 8.5% for R-CDs, respectively. Optical and structural characterizations of these CDs show that the variation of sp 2-sp 3 hybridized domains resulted from the elements doping (S, N) fundamentally determines the color of CDs. The highest FLQYs of B-CDs in all CDs gives it an advantage to detect metal ions, which finds strong quenching only toward Ag+ because of the interaction between Ag+ and the cores. Likewise, these multicolor CDs attempt to fabricate CDs@PVP composite film emitted white-light as well as a clad material of WLED devices. The results indicate that the correlated color temperatures (CCT) in the ranges of cold and warm white lights can be obtained with different color rendering indexes (CRI) and luminous efficacies. Moreover, CCT and CRI are nearly up to the extremum at the same current value with increasing currents, implying that such CDs are potential as a kind of fluorescent nanomaterial to applicate to WLED device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S, N co-doping
Multi-color emissions
Ag+ quenching
LED device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of S and N co-doped CDs
Construction of CDs/PVP films and the WLED based on CDs/PVP
Synthesis, optical and structural properties of the S, N-CDs
WLED preparation based on the multicolor CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of synthesizing tricolor CDs by solvothermal treatment (ethanol) of CA and 2AT. (b) Photographs of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV (right) irradiation.

(a–c) UV–vis (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of the typical tricolor CDs in sequence (the inset photographs are obtained under optimal excitation irradiation). (d–f) The corresponding excitation-emission matrix for B-CDs (d), G-CDs (e) and R-CDs (f) respectively.
TEM images of blue(a), green(b), and red(c) emitted CDs, the insets from top to bottom are the corresponding size distribution, HRTEM and FFT diffraction pattern of carbon lattice of these CDs.
The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and S2p in blue(a), green(b), and red(c) emitted CDs.
Raman (a), FT-IR (b) and fluorescence decay curves (c) of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs.

(a)The comparison of adding various metal ions with the same concentration of 1 M in B-CDs solutions; (b)The fluorescence intensities of B-CDs solutions with different concentrations of Ag+ added at the excitation wavelength of 435 nm; (c)Graph of the linear relationship between the concentration CAg+ and F 0/F, which is derived from the results in (b) and executed the Stern–Volmer fitting. (d–f) FT-IR, absorption and lifetime spectra of B-CDs with Ag+.

(a) B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs PVP composite films under 365 nm UV excitation as well as the mixed white-light PL spectrum and the corresponding PL photographs is shown in the inset. (b) Calculated CIE coordinates based on the PL spectra of the prepared WLED in the light of the volume ratio of R-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs solutions are 3:2:1, 2:3:1, 1:2:3, 1:1:2 and 1:1:3, respectively. (c) EL spectra of the WLED with CCT = 4021 K in different currents, corresponding CRI and CCT changes showed in inset.
Content of sectional chemical bonds in blue, green, and red emitted CDs respectively.
Fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curve of the different CDs.
Comparison of detection performance of different fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection.

entry.bold: N, S-CDs (A1P and TH doped, B-CDs)

head.ce:title: Multicolor carbon dots: Induced by sp2-sp 3 hybridized domains and their application in ion detection and WLED

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zibo Huatian Rubber & Plastic Technology Co., Ltd, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zibo Huatian Rubber & Plastic Technology Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zibo Huatian Rubber & Plastic Technology Co., Ltd

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The heteroatom doping to achieve carbon dots has played a crucial part in exploring the fluorescence mechanism of multicolor emissions, which is closely related with the application development of such materials. Herein, a facile method was developed to synthesize three-color CDs via the co-doping of S and N elements with the assistance of ethanol solvent. The achieved CDs mainly emit blue, green and red lights, yet the photoluminescence quantum yields (FLQYs) generally decreases from 54.0% for B-CDs, 11.5% for G-CDs to 8.5% for R-CDs, respectively. Optical and structural characterizations of these CDs show that the variation of sp 2-sp 3 hybridized domains resulted from the elements doping (S, N) fundamentally determines the color of CDs. The highest FLQYs of B-CDs in all CDs gives it an advantage to detect metal ions, which finds strong quenching only toward Ag+ because of the interaction between Ag+ and the cores. Likewise, these multicolor CDs attempt to fabricate CDs@PVP composite film emitted white-light as well as a clad material of WLED devices. The results indicate that the correlated color temperatures (CCT) in the ranges of cold and warm white lights can be obtained with different color rendering indexes (CRI) and luminous efficacies. Moreover, CCT and CRI are nearly up to the extremum at the same current value with increasing currents, implying that such CDs are potential as a kind of fluorescent nanomaterial to applicate to WLED device.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging star of fluorescent nanomaterials, have received increasing attention by dint of the unique merits, such as optical properties, antiphotobleaching, biocompatibility, and so on [1–4]. For years’ efforts on synthesizing thousands of CDs with different luminous colors and effects [5–7], CDs materials have been considered promising candidate in the application of photocatalysis, ion detection, biosensing and cell labeling, etc [8–11]. However, some fatal problems still remains, for instance, CDs emitted efficiently in red light wavelength region are scarce, not to mention high efficiency full-color-emitting, which severely hinders their application in the biomedical and white-light LED [12]. Additionally, the reality at present is that there is always the controversy on the photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms of these multicolor CDs and results in being unable to get unified view from diverse luminescent phenomena in the literatures [13–15], which fundamentally raises the difficulty to build a full-color emission spectrum from CDs materials and expand these materials to broader applications.

To date, it has been an efficient method to induce long-wavelength and multicolor emission by doping heteroatoms into the lattices of carbon, leading to defects with the framework of the CDs and contracting the band gaps, which cause further redshift of the fluorescence. Among all of the doped elements, it turns out that nitrogen(N)-doping is more appropriate and effective because of the similar atom size of N and C, which is in favour of the formation of strong valence bonds between each other [16]. The N-doping CDs (dopant tris(hydroxylmethyl)aminomethane) with the procedure of microwave synthesis has even achieved a fluorescence quantum yields (FLQYs) of 99% even [17]. Inspired by the phenomenon above, recent research focuses on the doping of N element in the form of –NH2 and –NO2 groups assisted with a variety of solvents to achieve the multicolor CDs. Lin et al. [18] used three isomerized phenylenediamine to synthesize tri-color emitted CDs assisted with ethanol solution, which had been a pioneer for the multicolor fluorescence of CDs. Other –NH2 containing reagents, urea or l-glutamic acid together with phenylenediamine, synthesized respectively multicolor CDs with more significant optical performance [19,20]. Besides that both 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and 3,4,9,10-nitroperylene inlaying different amounts of –NO2 groups have also obtained multicolor CDs possessing the highest FLQYs of green and red emissions so far [21,22], while technological processes to synthesize these CDs are tedious and harsh for the industrial preparation. The pace to search multicolor-emissive CDs never quits. According to the theory of energy band, heteroatom sulfur (S) by means of doping would further narrow the energy gap compared to N-doping on account of a lower electronegativity for S element [23], which is more beneficial to obtain long-wavelength fluorescence emission of the CDs. In fact, sulfur element has played an irreplaceable role in boosting FLQYs of CDs via S and N elements co-doped means in some reports [24,25]. Additionally, introduced S-containing groups (e.g. –SH) as new functional groups might provide more possibilities for these CDs. For example, the fluorescence of CDs (S and N co-doping) were strengthened with added Ag+ on account of the strong affinity from S-containing groups and metal cations [26], making CDs the best candidate materials for exploring the mechanism of metal ions quenching. Moreover, several reports have demonstrated to successfully synthesize S, N-doped CDs, and indeed be able to emit orange or red fluorescence [27–29], yet they did not show efficient fluorescence in the long-wavelength regions. In fact, little work has been devoted to achieve multicolor and full-color CDs via S and N co-doping to date, the synergistic effect of them may endows the S, N co-doping CDs with unexpected performance. Therefore, it is of great significance and also an enormous challenge to search the PL mechanism of S, N-doped CDs with multicolor emissions.

We proposed a one-step solvothermal method to prepare S, N co-doped CDs (S, N-CDs) emitted colorful fluorescence, which was developed by using 2-aminothiophenol (2AT) as the N and S dopant with the assistance of ethanol solvents. Citric acid (CA) is also as carbon source since CA can be combined with multiple dopants to synthesize the CDs with multicolor emissions [30–32]. The prepared CDs could emit fluorescence from blue to red color, and the highest FLQYs reach up to 54.0% for B-CDs, which possibly results from the variation of sp 2-sp 3 hybridized domains while S and N elements are doped in the form of different groups speculated from the multiple characterizations. In light of the highest FLQYs of B-CDs in all the three kinds of CDs, they were employed to monitor metal ions by means of quenching fluorescence. The results indicated that the strongest quenching only occurs in the presence of Ag+, which due to the interaction between Ag+ and the cores and proved by the FT-IR and absorption spectroscopy. Additionally, these multicolor CDs were adopted to fabricate the CDs/PVP composite film and further used as a clad material of WLED devices. Photometric and colorimetric studies found that CCT in the ranges of cold and warm white lights could be obtained with the maximal parameters of CRI = 80.1, LE = 2.38 lm/W. With different current input to a WLED with CCT = 4021 K, CCT and CRI nearly show a synchronicity with the increasing currents, and all of the results were elaborated described in Section III in detail.

2AT and CA were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China), ethanol solvents and PVP powder were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Company of Tianjin city in China. Various metal cations reagents were prepared by dissolving MgCl2·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, AgNO3, AlCl3·6H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, NaCl, KCl, BaCl2, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, ZnCl2, and CuSO4·5H2O into ultrapure water. All of the used chemicals, solvents, and the reagents at least reached analytical grade. In all experiments De-ionized water was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S and N co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The reactions at different conditions were conducted by one-step solvothermal process to obtain varying colors of CDs. For blue and green CDs: CA (0.75 mmol) and 2AT (0.25 mmol) were dissolved in ethanol of 10 ml. After stirring to thoroughly clear solution at the room temperature, the mixture was poured in Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 170 and 200 °C respectively for 12 h for the formation of blue and green CDs. For red CDs: CA (0.75 mmol) and 2AT (0.75 mmol) were dissolved in ethanol of 10 ml with 20 μl concentrated sulfuric acid. As previously mentioned, the solution was poured in Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave (25 ml) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the reactors were naturally cooled to the room temperature, and the obtained solutions were collected by centrifugation at 10000 r min−1 for 5 min to remove the larger particles. After that, these solutions were condensed by rotary evaporation under 50 °C for 30 min, and continued to be dried over 24 h into powder (or colloid) using vacuum drying oven at 70 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of CDs/PVP films and the WLED based on CDs/PVP
ce:section.ce:para: CDs solutions of each color (9 ml) was mixed with 3 g PVP power by ultrasound at 90 °C for 0.5 h. Then, the above mixed solutions was quickly poured into Teflon molds, and further dried for 1 day, and finally obtain the film. Moreover, after repeated adjustments based on the above mixed solution the volume ratio of B-CDs:G-CDs:R-CDs = 1:2:3 was confirmed to obtain a white light film. Apply 50 μL of the CDs/PVP mixed solution into the cup-shaped optical lens, put it in room temperature to dry naturally for 24 h, and then place the cup-shaped optical lens coated with CDs/PVP on top of the UV chip.

A JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images was used to investigate the morphologies of the S, Nco-doped CDs. FTIR spectra were recorded (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, America) in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. XPS of CDs was performed using a PHI–5000C ESCA system (PerkinElmer Inc., USA) with Al Kα X-ray radiation. Raman spectra were recorded by a Laser Confocal Micro-Raman Spectroscopy using a 785 nm excitation line of a diode laser (Renishaw). Absorption spectrum was acquired by a TU-1901 UV/Visible spectrophotometer (Persee, Beijing). PL spectra, quantum yields, and fluorescence lifetimes of CDs were performed on a new type of FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, England).

To evaluate the selectivity of prepared B-CDs with the highest QY, eleven kinds of solutions with different metal cations, including Ag+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ba2+ and Cu2+ ions, were prepared respectively with a concentration of 1 M. Then 20 μL of the above metal ion solution was mixed with 2 ml of B-CDs solution. After 20 min, fluorescence emission spectra of the corresponding supernatants were recorded using the excitation wavelength at 435 nm for comparison. In view of the best quenching to Ag+ in all of the above metal ions, different concentrations of Ag+ (0–1 nM, 20 μL) were examined to explore the influence of quenching to them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, optical and structural properties of the S, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The multicolor-emitting S, N-CDs was prepared by solvothermal decomposition and resynthesis of 2AT and CA dissolved in ethanol as shown in Fig. 1 a. In view of reaction conditions including temperature, time and molar ratio which can easily influence the processes of thermal treatment, hence, all of these were optimized to achieve bright colors of CDs solutions. Among that, the CDs solutions with blue, green and red emissions under 365 nm UV-irradiation are used as a typical to be shown in Fig. 1b, which are noted as B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs orderly in consideration of their emissive colors and further studied in the following sections.

B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs solutions severally exhibit remarkable absorption in 200–300 nm−1 region, yet weak in 300–500 nm−1 region in Fig. 2 a–c (purple spectrum on the left side). In the 200-300 nm−1 region, there are five peaks observed at 207, 220, 250, 280 and 291 nm−1 respectively, which are mainly induced by the π-π* transition of the CC bands in carbon cores as well as sp 2-hybridized carbon nanodomains embedded in a matrix composed of sp 3-hybridized carbon on the margin of cores [33,34]. Although the others also undergo slight variations in the intensity, the most obvious change is the intensity inversion of the peaks at 207 and 220 nm−1 along with the varying colors from blue to red. In the region of 300–500 nm−1, a broad absorption peak, generally attributed to the n-π* transition of the conjugated CO/CN/CS bonds on the surface of the core [35,36], seems to move to higher wavenumbers for the R-CDs. Likewise, Fig. 2a–c shows the optimal fluorescence excitation (Ex) spectra and fluorescence emission (Em) spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs respectively, and corresponding photographs of each CDs solutions are also exhibited in the insets. It is clear that excitation spectrum of B-CDs is composed of several peaks: two or three weak peaks appeared alongside the main peak. Fig. 2d exhibits the variation of emission centers under the combined effects of these peaks in excitation spectrum, resulting in be excitation-dependent for the emission of B-CDs. For the G-CDs in Fig. 2b, both excitation and emission spectra are still with the feature of multi-peaks, crowding together and indistinguishable, which makes two emission centers connect each other as shown in Fig. 2e, and main emission center (Ex = 475 nm, Em = 540 nm) remains excitation-dependent strictly. For the R-CDs in Fig. 2c, excitation and emission spectra become a simple singlet, yet emission spectrum dramatically broadens compared to that of B-CDs and G-CDs. The excitation-emission matrix of R-CDs in Fig. 2f illustrates only an emission center sprawling with the more remarkable excitation-dependent feature, implying that the red emission of R-CDs is completely different from the blue and green CDs. Considering that the FLQYs has been a decisive indicator of CDs materials in various optical applications, the absolute FLQYs of these CDs were decided to be 54.0% for B-CDs, 11.5% for G-CDs and 8.5% for R-CDs, respectively. Obviously, 8.5% of the R-CDs here is relatively unsatisfactory although such S and N co-doped CDs with red emission have not achieved high FLQYs up to now [37,38].

Microscopic morphology and structure of the CDs with different colors can provide other information on the insight into their carbogenic cores as well as surfaces. Fig. 3 a–c shows the CDs are almost spherical and homogeneous in size. According to the color of CDs, histograms illustrate the average diameters are 7.0, 5.7 and 4.2 nm for these CDs as shown in the upper-right corner insets of Fig. 3a–c, while HRTEM images of corresponding CDs exhibit the well-resolved lattice fringes in the middle-right insets of Fig. 3a–c, and the average lattice spacing were calculated to be 0.21 nm for all the CDs, which agrees with (100) graphitic carbon [39]. FFT diffraction patterns of carbon lattices of these CDs further suggest the increasingly trend toward single-crystal structure with the color from blue to red. To further understand the surface states of these CDs, XPS spectra were also adopted as shown in fig. s1, all of which reveal C, N, O and S elements via different groups. Detailed in Fig. 4 a–c, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be fitted well to four binding energy peaks: 284.3, 285.1, 286.3 and 288.6 eV, correspondingly assigned to CC/C–C, C–O/C–S/C–N, CN/CO and COOH, respectively. It is hardly able to fit the spectrum of N 1s, which is made an attempt to deconvolute into two peaks at 400.0 and 401.2 eV, attributed to pyridinic/pyrrolic N and graphite N groups respectively. Likewise, S 2p spectrum generally should be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 163.8 and 164.9 eV, which are assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of C–S–C covalent bond except for the C-SOx-C (x = 2, 3, 4, …) bond at 169.1 eV for R-CDs due to the addition of dilute sulfuric acid. In Table 1 , the content of C–C/CC bond increases from 30.83% to 43.87%, while C–O/C–N/C–S peak correspondingly decreases from 43.08% to 30.04%, indicating sp 2/sp 3 (the parameter to illustrate the degree of graphitization in carbogenic cores) increases and agreeing with the FFT diffraction patterns of carbon lattices of these CDs, that is more high graphitization in R-CDs. Moreover, graphite N also increases sharply from 29.56% to 63.63% when pyridinic/pyrrolic N decreases dramatically from 70.44% to 36.37% with the color changes of these CDs. The S elements are also different contents in the different colors of CDs in the form of C–S–C covalent bond. According to the “size effect” [40], the CDs with long-wavelength emission often possesses a bigger core compared to those of the CDs emitting short-wavelength fluorescence, obviously, gradually shrunken cores from B-CDs to R-CDs are inconsistent with this theory. However, the report on multi-color emissive CDs proved that graphitic N was further increased to trigger red fluorescence [41], which parallels with the above results from XPS. In view of the recent report that sp 2-sp 3 hybridized domains played a key role in determining color features of CDs [42]. Therefore, here is a possible proposal that sp 2-sp 3 hybridized domains via S and N co-doping fundamentally determine the optical properties of these CDs although plentiful surface states composed of O, H, S and N elements by the surface groups may be beneficial for multi-color emissions of CDs.

The increasing degrees of graphitization with the colors from blue to red can be verified by the GD value (the intensity ratio of G and D peaks): 0.83 for B-CDs, 1.05 for G-CDs and 1.20 for R-CDs in Fig. 5 a. FT-IR spectroscopy also was adopted to thoroughly investigate the functional groups of these CDs as shown in Fig. 5b. Observing from left to right, a series of absorption peaks at approximately 2850–3400 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H/C–H/N–H, and nearly remains stable in three kinds of CDs, suggesting related surface groups (-OH, –NH2 or –CH3) do not have a change in different colors of CDs. Sequentially, several typical absorption bands such as C–O/CO/C–N/C–S, COOH stretching and amido CONH bending appear in 1050–1800 cm−1. Discovered by comparison, however, a weak CC/CN peak abruptly comes at 1600 cm−1, and a battery of C–O(N/S)–C vibrations in 600–1050 cm−1 also strengthen for some peaks, yet C–N/C–S peak at around 1300 cm−1 seems to be vanished for R-CDs, providing powerful evidences to illustrate the stronger graphitization and different polyaromatic structures, thereby, resulting in different fluorescent colors as proved by the above results. To further understand the relationship between structure and optical feature, time-resolved PL decay curves of three kinds of CDs were achieved to detect the fluorescence lifetimes as exhibited in Fig. 5c. As listed in Table 2 , three curves of the different CDs can be matched by a double exponential formula including short-lived τ1 and long-lived τ2. The τ2 is on behalf of the recombination of cores onto surface states [43], which predominates for the effectual relaxation of carriers due to the higher proportion, and its proportion generally increases from B-CDs to R-CDs, yet decreasing for the value itself. In view of the significant excitation-dependent emissions of these CDs, another τ1 should be attributed to surface state, its fitted value and content gradually decreases from 5.3, 20.7% in B-CDs to 2.1, 10.3% in R-CDs, which is related possibly to decreasing surface area of the core. Therefore, component τ2 plays dominant roles for the optical features of these CDs, and gets very poor with the only value of 4.3 ns to foreground the component τ1, which results in energy absorption related to surface states enhanced slightly in R-CDs.

Fluorescent quenching by the metal ions, the prepared optimally CDs materials have been applied to detect metal ions such as Cu2+, Hg2+, and Ag+, etc [44–46]. B-CDs possesses the highest FLQYs among three colors of CDs and has an advantage to explore the quenching mechanism toward various metal ions as shown in Fig. 6 a. It can be seen that Ag+ has the largest capable of quenching B-CDs solution, having the potential for B-CDs to detect Ag+ in different application areas. To further understand the quenching rule toward Ag+, Fig. 6b lists the influence of silver ion on the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs solutions (the concentrations of Ag+ is within 0–1 M), which were transformed into the two-dimensional coordinate of F0/F vs. CAg+ (F0 and F: fluorescent intensity as well as added metal ion, CAg+: ionic concentration) in Fig. 6c. Obviously, these data are a linear rule and can be fitted by Stern-Volmer dynamic quenching equation of
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
C = 1 +
K
s v
C
(kq: quenching rate constant, τ0: lifetime of the fluorophore, C: concentration of quencher, and Ksv: quenching constant) [47]. Nevertheless, there exists an inflection point at CAg+ = 3 μM as shown in the inset of Fig. 6c, implying different quenching rule in the concentration region less than 3 μM. In the same manner, F0/F vs. CAg+ consistent with another equation of y = 0.0396x+1.0309 (R2 = 0.9993) in this region and obtained the limit of detection (LOD = 3σ/m) of 0.32 μM for B-CDs-Ag+ system, thereby, which achieves considerable effects compared with other several reports on Ag+ detection using fluorescent material as listed in Table 3 [48–50].

The quenching mechanism of different CDs toward Ag+ has always been a interesting subject in scientific community and several opinions had been proposed including the affinity, oxidation-reduction and energy/electron exchange between Ag+ and the core or the functional groups (-OH, –COOH, –NH2 and –SH) on the surface of CDs [58–60]. The FT-IR, absorption and fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs are compared with the same case only added moderate Ag+ as shown in Fig. 6d–f. After the addition of Ag+ in Fig. 6d, the peak at ca.1350 cm−1, mostly attributed to hydroxyl vibration, relatively strengthens and becomes a most notable feature of the whole spectrum besides some weak changes from the C–N and C–O(N/S)–C groups related to the sp 2-sp 3 domains in cores. Likewise, absorption peak at ca. 220 nm−1, closely associated with the core states, dramatically weakens and the peak related to surface state in 300–400 nm−1 is also flooded into the baseline after the addition of Ag+. In addition, component τ2 also decreases from 79.3% to 52.5% by fitted the PL decay curve of B-CDs + Ag+ system. Therefore, a proposal should be made that the quenching of B-CDs along with adding Ag+ stems is because of the interaction between the cores and Ag+ ions via the surface groups, which lays the theoretical foundation for the application of B-CDs materials in metal ions detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: WLED preparation based on the multicolor CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a promising substitute of traditional phosphors, multicolor CDs composited polymer with PVA, PVP, and PPMA have been attempted to applied in solid lighting of LED [61,62]. Herein, three kinds of as-prepared CDs were also composited severally with PVP in solutions, and were mixed together to fabricate WLED of CDs/PVP films based on the color mixing principle as shown in Fig. 7 a. Blue-light region in the spectrum remains stable with a peak at ca. 450 nm, yet green and red color seems to be combined into a broad peaks at 570 nm although corresponding film is white visually under UV radiation. The volume ratio of R-CDs: G-CDs: B-CDs = 3:2:1, 2:3:1, 1:2:3, 1:1:2 and 1:1:3 were attempted, and the ratio of 1:2:3was achieved white light emission placed on the top of the LED chip (ultraviolet light is emitted when electricity is turned on) as a coating film and performed photometry and chromaticity measurements after dried. As shown in Fig. 7b, the as-synthesized WLEDs show color coordinates of x = 0.33–0.39 and y = 0.36–0.41 with a correlated color temperatures (CCT) of 3867–5703 K, color rendering indexes (CRI) of 78.2–80.1 and the luminous efficacies (LE) of 1.67–2.38 lm/W. Therein, the fourth WLED obtains x, y=(0.38, 0.40), CRI = 78.3 and LE = 2.08 lm/W, performing better than those of other CD-based WLEDs due to the CCT = 4021 K closed to warm white light (CCT<4500 K). Fig. 7c shows the electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the fourth WLED under different driving currents from 20 to 300 mA. With increasing current, the EL spectral intensities generally weaken while the pattern of the EL spectrum remains unchanged. It can be seen that CRI and CCT glow, appear an inflexion at 120–180 mA with the maximum of CRI = 80.7, CCT = 4650 K, and later decrease with increasing currents as shown in the inset of Fig. 7c. These results illustrate that as-prepared multicolor CDs possess great application prospect in fabricating CDs-based lighting devices of WLED with warm white light although more comprehensive exploring is imperative to discover deeper information including the improving CRI, CCT, and LE, etc.

In summary, we have adopted a facile strategy of S and N co-doping and adjusting the reaction conditions of precursors with the aid of ethanol solvent to prepare multicolor CDs. As-prepared CDs emitted blue, green and red fluorescence respectively, but its FLQYs generally decreased with varying colors. After detailed characterization on optics and structure, we believe that sp 2-sp 3 hybridized domains influenced by S and N co-doping determine the color of these CDs. Taking into consideration of the highest FLQYs of B-CDs in all CDs, we performed the detection of metal ions utilizing quenching mechanism, which achieved only in addition of Ag+ resulted from interaction between Ag+ and the cores. Following the color mixing principle, we adopted three-colors CDs solution to fabricate the CDs/PVP composite film, moreover, which were applicated to the clad materials of WLED devices. CCT in the ranges of cold and warm white lights obtained with the best CRI = 80.1, LE = 2.38 lm/W. For a WLED in warm-white-light region, CCT and CRI almost simultaneously appeared extremum with increasing currents, providing a convenient path for product optimization.

Weijie Ren: with assistance from. Jingjing Bai: Formal analysis, conducted the experiments and analyzed the results. Tingting Cai: and. Shijia Li: and. Ernan Pang: suggestionsuggestions about the experiments, Jun Zhang; and. Zhenzhong Li: Supervision, supervised the study and designed the experiments. All authors contributed to the discussion and interpretation of the results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fund for Shanxi ‘1331 Project’ Collaborative Innovation Center, and the Fund for Shanxi ‘1331 Project’ Key Subjects Construction.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2021.111064.
